Commentary: CHRYSOSTOM: He said not, Blessed are the poor in goods, but poor in spirit; that thou mightest know that it is the humility of the mind, not the necessity of poverty, that He rewards. AUGUSTINE: Or, poor in spirit means poor in sins, that is, those who have cast sin out of their hearts. For the spirit sometimes signifies pride, as the Apostle says, I shall come and know the power of those who are puffed up, not their speech. AMBROSE: But poverty of spirit is not a defect of sense, but an abstinence of pride and pomp. And well is the kingdom of heaven promised to the poor in spirit, for they who have not sought earthly kingdoms are worthy to receive the heavenly.

CHRYSOSTOM: Having exhorted to humility, He proceeds to a second virtue, patience under adversity; for He says, Blessed are the meek. AUGUSTINE: Mourning is grief for the loss of things held dear. But those who are converted to God lose the things which they were wont to embrace as dear in this world. And forasmuch as they cannot yet despise these things entirely, they are afflicted with a certain grief.

CHRYSOSTOM: When He says Blessed are the meek, He means not those who are never moved, but those who, though moved, order their passion with reason and the fear of God. AUGUSTINE: Those who are meek shall inherit the earth, that is, the Church; for the Church is indeed a sort of earth upon which the meek tread, being purified from all things carnal. Or the earth is the solidity and firmness of the eternal inheritance.

CHRYSOSTOM: See how He has here reversed the order of nature. For they who hunger and thirst after earthly things are miserable; but He pronounces those blessed who hunger after righteousness. JEROME: Not righteousness in a common sense, but with the same ardour with which one who hungers and thirsts after food and drink. For the righteousness of God consists in this, that the soul, having despised earthly things, should pant after divine things.

AUGUSTINE: The merciful in their disposition obtain mercy for themselves; by forgiving others, they obtain forgiveness for themselves. Hence He says, Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy. CHRYSOSTOM: He that shows mercy to others shall himself receive mercy from God, who requires of us nothing great, but that we should be kind to our fellow servants, and that He should have occasion to show His mercy to us.

AUGUSTINE: Clean of heart are those in whom there is no double heart, that is, who aim at pleasing God singly and with all their purpose. CHRYSOSTOM: The pure heart is that which is not stained by any evil thought. And this is necessary for seeing God; for as the eye needs to be clear and sound to see the sun, so likewise the soul needs to be pure and without spot to see God.

CHRYSOSTOM: After healing our passions by gentleness and mourning and meekness, He next promotes us to the love of our neighbour, through mercy and purity. AUGUSTINE: Peacemakers in themselves are those who, by ordering and directing all the motions of their soul, and subjecting them to reason, that is, to the mind and spirit, and by having their carnal lusts thoroughly subdued, have become a kingdom of God.

JEROME: Lest we should think that it is only voluntary poverty of spirit and righteous mourning that is blessed, He adds also those who suffer persecution. CHRYSOSTOM: He has made this last beatitude a climax, as it were a crown of all the virtues before mentioned. For he is not merely meek or merciful or pure in heart who takes in good part the reproaches and the suffering of his adversaries.
